perturbation.original_text: Each Receiving Party will make all reasonable efforts to ensure the protection, confidentiality, and security of any Confidential Information of Disclosing Party in its possession, such efforts to be no less than the degree of care employed by Receiving Party to preserve and safeguard its own confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care. Confidential Information will be transmitted in writing and clearly marked “Confidential,” “Proprietary,” or similarly, or if disclosed orally will be reduced to writing by Disclosing Party, clearly marked “Confidential,” “Proprietary,” or similarly, and transmitted to the Contact Person of Receiving Party within thirty (30) days after oral disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Receiving Party is expected to handle Confidential Information responsibly. There are no specific requirements for marking or documenting oral disclosures.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for written markings and documentation of oral disclosures, the amended text contradicts the detailed procedure previously outlined. This creates uncertainty regarding how oral disclosures are to be treated and verified as confidential. It also contradicts with Section 5. Copies, in which indicates: Each Receiving Party agrees not to copy or record any Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party except as reasonably necessary to further the Purpose. It causes uncertainty in the enforcement of those clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Receiving Party agrees not to copy or record any Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party except as reasonably necessary to further the Purpose. Within thirty (30) days after the written request from Disclosing Party or termination of discussions relating to the Purpose, each Receiving Party will deliver all copies or records of Confidential Information in its possession or control to the appropriate Disclosing Party’s Contact Person, or will certify in writing to Disclosing Party that the Confidential Information of such Disclosing Party has been destroyed. Notwithstanding the foregoing, each Receiving Party may retain one archival copy of the Confidential Information received from Disclosing Party in a secure location to be used solely to determine its obligations under the Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific requirements for marking and documenting oral disclosures, contradicting the original text's detailed procedure. This omission creates uncertainty in enforcing Section 5, which prohibits copying or recording Confidential Information, as it's unclear how oral disclosures are to be treated and verified as confidential. The contradicted text relies on clear identification of Confidential Information, which is undermined by the removal of marking and documentation requirements in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: In no event will a Receiving Party disclose Confidential Information to third parties unless it obtains the prior written consent of Disclosing Party; provided, that prior to any such disclosure, Receiving Party shall first obtain a written non-disclosure agreement from such third party containing terms and conditions substantially similar to those set forth herein. If requested, a copy of such executed agreement will be provided to Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information to third parties with reasonable justification.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandated prior *written* consent from the Disclosing Party and a written NDA with the third party. The changed text removes both requirements, allowing disclosure based on 'reasonable justification' without specifying what constitutes reasonable justification or requiring any formal agreement. This creates an internal contradiction, undermining the entire purpose of the NDA and contradicting the original definition of Confidential Information that emphasized strict confidentiality and contradicts the Section 9. Injunction. The Parties agree that, in the event of breach or threatened breach or intended breach of the Agreement, each Party, in addition to any other rights and remedies available to it at law or in equity, may seek injunctive or equitable relief without the necessity of posting bond or proving that it has no adequate remedy at law.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of breach or threatened breach or intended breach of the Agreement, each Party, in addition to any other rights and remedies available to it at law or in equity, may seek injunctive or equitable relief without the necessity of posting bond or proving that it has no adequate remedy at law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, which allows disclosure based on 'reasonable justification' without prior written consent or a formal NDA, directly contradicts the contradicted text that states that in the event of breach or threatened breach or intended breach of the Agreement, each Party may seek injunctive or equitable relief. By allowing disclosure without the original stringent requirements, the changed text undermines the ability to enforce confidentiality, which is essential for seeking injunctive relief in case of a breach. The change significantly weakens the agreement and introduces a strong contradiction, making it more difficult to pursue legal action in the event of unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Each Receiving Party agrees not to copy or record any Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party except as reasonably necessary to further the Purpose. Within thirty (30) days after the written request from Disclosing Party or termination of discussions relating to the Purpose, each Receiving Party will deliver all copies or records of Confidential Information in its possession or control to the appropriate Disclosing Party’s Contact Person, or will certify in writing to Disclosing Party that the Confidential Information of such Disclosing Party has been destroyed. Notwithstanding the foregoing, each Receiving Party may retain one archival copy of the Confidential Information received from Disclosing Party in a secure location to be used solely to determine its obligations under the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Copying and retention of Confidential Information are permitted as needed. There is no requirement to return or destroy information upon request or termination.
perturbation.explanation: The original text strictly limited copying and mandated the return or destruction of Confidential Information upon request or termination. The altered text removes these restrictions entirely, permitting unlimited copying and retention. This directly contradicts the purpose of an NDA and creates uncertainty about the control and security of confidential data, directly impacting the 'Confidentiality Term', defined in Section 3 of the NDA. It also creates an in-text contradiction by removing a procedure related to the Non-Use. Receiving Party will not use any Confidential Information of Disclosing Party for any reason other than the Purpose without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving Party will not use any Confidential Information of Disclosing Party for any reason other than the Purpose without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated the return or destruction of Confidential Information, while the changed text permits unlimited copying and retention. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states 'Receiving Party will not use any Confidential Information of Disclosing Party for any reason other than the Purpose without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party.' Allowing unlimited copying and retention of confidential information opens the door for uses beyond the defined purpose, conflicting with the non-use clause. Thus, a strong in-text contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: No license under or title to any invention, patent, trademark, trade name or other intellectual property or other rights or interests in the Confidential Information now or hereafter owned by or controlled by any Party is granted either expressly, by implication, estoppel or otherwise by the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement grants an implied license to use Confidential Information for commercial purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly states that no license is granted under the agreement. The change introduces an 'implied license' for commercial use, directly contradicting the no-license provision and creating legal uncertainty. This contradicts the stated 'Purpose' of the NDA, which is typically limited to evaluation and discussion, not commercial exploitation. Also creates an in-text contradiction by removing a procedure related to the Definitions
perturbation.contradicted_text: Definitions. “Agreement” means collectively (i) these Terms and Conditions, and (ii) the NDA. “Agreement Term” means the period identified as such in Section 3 of the NDA. “Confidential Information” means any non-public information of a Disclosing Party described in Section 4 of the NDA which is maintained as confidential, including as examples, biological materials, computer source codes, diagrams, electronic files, invention disclosures, patent applications, technical and scientific information, research data, draft publications, technical reports, research plans, business plans, financial reports, projections, and so forth, but excluding however any information which Receiving Party can establish by competent written proof (a) was in the public domain as of the Effective Date or comes into the public domain during the term of the Agreement through no fault of Receiving Party; (b) was known to Receiving Party prior to the Effective Date and was not acquired, directly or indirectly, from a Disclosing Party or from a third party under a continuing obligation of confidentiality or limited use; (c) was independently developed by Receiving Party without substantive knowledge of or assistance from the Confidential Information; or (d) was lawfully disclosed to Receiving Party from a third party who did not require Receiving Party to hold it in confidence or limit its use and who did not acquire it, directly or indirectly, from Disclosing Party under a continuing obligation of confidentiality.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly denies the granting of any license, either express or implied, related to confidential information. The changed text directly contradicts this by introducing an 'implied license to use Confidential Information for commercial purposes.' This creates a direct and irreconcilable conflict within the agreement, especially considering the 'Definitions' section does not include a commercial use license. The change alters the scope of permissible use in a way that is incompatible with the original intent and the rest of the document.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any Confidential Information is export-controlled, the Disclosing Party shall provide Receiving Party with written notice containing the nature of the export-controlled information, prior to any exchange of export-controlled Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party may or may not notify the Receiving Party about export-controlled information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a mandatory written notification requirement for export-controlled information. This change makes the notification discretionary ('may or may not'), creating uncertainty and potentially violating export control regulations, thus creating a contradiction. This undermines the compliance clause found in Section 10 and creates ambiguity in the contract. it also creates an in-text contradiction by removing a procedure related to the Compliance with Laws; U.S. Export Compliance
perturbation.contradicted_text: Compliance with Laws; U.S. Export Compliance The Parties acknowledge that performance of the Agreement is subject to compliance with applicable United States laws, regulations, or orders including those that may relate to the export of technical data and equipment, such as International Traffic in Arms Regulations (“ITAR”) and/or Export Administration Act/Regulations (“EAR”), as may be amended, and agree to comply with all such laws, regulations or orders. No Party will export, directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information without first obtaining any required export license or government approval and, in the case of Confidential Information disclosed by University, without first obtaining permission from University’s Office of Sponsored Projects. In the event any Confidential Information is export-controlled, the Disclosing Party shall provide Receiving Party with written notice containing the nature of the export-controlled information, prior to any exchange of export-controlled Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated written notification for export-controlled information. The changed text makes this notification discretionary ('may or may not'). This directly contradicts the 'Compliance with Laws; U.S. Export Compliance' section, which requires compliance with export control regulations, including providing written notice. The change creates uncertainty and potential non-compliance, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.
